CONTENT MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS AND SOCIAL MEDIA IN BUSINESS

Chapter 8 — Content Management Systems a — She writes the text. A volunteer finds the photo. Another volunteer reviews it for brand consistency. Someone schedules

beats.text: Consider what actually has to happen when Tracy publishes an adoption announcement. She writes the text. A volunteer finds the photo. Another volunteer reviews it for brand consistency. Someone schedules it to go live when traffic is highest. The same content needs to appear on Instagram, Facebook, and the website , but formatted differently for each platform. If someone comments with a question, someone has to answer, and that answer has to go somewhere.

segments.label: 01 / 12 · HOOK

title: THE CONTENT
LIFECYCLE.
segments.body_html: Organization.→Creation.→Storage.→Workflow.→Editing and versioning.

beats.text: The most useful way to understand what a CMS does is to trace what happens to a piece of content from the moment someone decides to create it to the moment it is removed or archived. This lifecycle has seven stages, and each one represents a problem the CMS solves. Organization. Before anyone writes anything, someone decides what they are trying to accomplish and why. What is this content for? Who will read it? What format does it need? This is the strategy stage.

segments.label: 02 / 12 · THE CONTENT LIFECYCLE

title: THE TRADE-OFF THAT DETERMINES WHICH
CMS TO CHOOSE.
segments.body_html:
—
Who Manages Hosting — Who Manages Hosting — first option applied to the chapter example.
Cost — Cost — first option applied to the chapter example.
Customization Ceiling — Customization Ceiling — first option applied to the chapter example.

beats.text: Every CMS sits on a spectrum between two poles: control and simplicity. Understanding the trade-off is what makes the choice clear. At the simplicity pole: WordPress.com (the hosted version), Squarespace, Wix. You sign up, choose a template, start publishing. You do not manage servers, install updates, or hire developers. You are up and running in an afternoon. The cost is lock-in: you do not own the infrastructure. You are renting a publishing service. If the platform changes pricing, discontinues a feature, or shuts down, you are affected.

segments.label: 03 / 12 · THE TRADE-OFF THAT DETERMINES

segments.section: SOCIAL MEDIA IS NOT ONE THING
title: SOCIAL MEDIA IS NOT
ONE THING.
segments.body_html:
—
Instagram — Instagram — content format that performs applied to the chapter example.
Facebook — Facebook — content format that performs applied to the chapter example.
TikTok — TikTok — content format that performs applied to the chapter example.

beats.text: Social media strategy is not "post on every platform." That sentence sounds like a plan. It is not. It is the absence of a plan. The reason is that different platforms have different audiences with different habits, and content that performs on one fails completely on another. The photo of Buddy the golden retriever got 47 likes on Instagram and zero engagement on LinkedIn , not because LinkedIn users dislike golden retrievers, but because LinkedIn users are on LinkedIn for professional networking, not pet adoption browsing.

segments.label: 04 / 12 · SOCIAL MEDIA IS NOT ONE THING

title: THE CONTENT CALENDAR AS
COORDINATION TOOL.
segments.body_html:
—
For A Nonprofit'S One-Week Social Media Plan — For A Nonprofit'S One-Week Social Media Plan — platform applied to the ch

beats.text: A content calendar is not a list of post ideas. It is a coordination tool that solves three organizational problems simultaneously: it keeps the team aligned on what is being posted and when, it forces advance planning that prevents last-minute scrambling, and it creates the structural spacing between posts that the platforms' algorithms reward. A basic content calendar is a spreadsheet. Each row is a post.

segments.label: 05 / 12 · THE CONTENT CALENDAR AS COORDI

segments.section: CLAUDE AS A CONTENT DRAFTING T
title: CLAUDE AS A CONTENT
DRAFTING TOOL.
segments.body_html: Draft from a description.→Adapt to platform.→Generate multiple lengths simultaneously.→Build calendar structure.→What Claude cannot do.

beats.text: Tracy has a Tuesday morning list: a 1,000-word blog post, three Instagram captions, a Facebook post, an email newsletter, and two TikTok captions. Without assistance, this is six hours of work. With Claude, it is forty-five minutes , if she uses it correctly. The distinction matters: Claude is a drafting engine, not a publishing engine. It generates first drafts quickly. It cannot verify that those drafts are accurate, authentic to the brand, or appropriate for the specific audience Tracy has built over five years.

segments.label: 06 / 12 · CLAUDE AS A CONTENT DRAFTING T

title: THE WORKFLOW THAT CONNECTS
THE PIECES.
segments.body_html:
The three systems in this chapter — CMS, social media strategy, and AI-assisted drafting — are not independent. They are

The Workflow That Connects the Pieces — The pipeline starts with goals: what does the organization need to accomplish? For Happy Tails, the goals are adoptions,

beats.text: The three systems in this chapter , CMS, social media strategy, and AI-assisted drafting , are not independent. They are stages in a single production pipeline. The pipeline starts with goals: what does the organization need to accomplish? For Happy Tails, the goals are adoptions, donations, and volunteer recruitment.

segments.label: 07 / 12 · THE WORKFLOW THAT CONNECTS THE

title: EXERCISES
segments.body_html: 1.→2.→3.→4.→5.

beats.text: Look at any organization's website , a local business, a nonprofit, a club. Without logging in, identify three things that would be difficult or impossible to update without a CMS: something that clearly changes frequently (events, available inventory, team members), something that appears consistently across multiple pages (a logo, a navigation bar, a footer), and something that suggests a workflow happened before it was published (a polished blog post, a formatted product description). For each, name the stage of the content lifecycle that the CMS is handling.

segments.label: 08 / 12 · EXERCISES

Add the framing"Answer as if you're a Xerox PARC engineer in 1980 explaining OfficeTalk to a visiting executive who has never seen mult

beats.text: The ideas in this chapter didn't appear from nowhere. Clarence Ellis was working on OfficeTalk at Xerox PARC , the first concurrent collaborative office system, the direct ancestor of every Google Docs, Slack, and Microsoft Teams workflow you'll touch this week decades before most people had heard of content management and collaborative software. Here's a prompt to find out more , and then make it better.
